name: Recruitment Stringency and Event-Yield Expectations
consequences: Tightening inclusion criteria (e.g., requiring ≥4 episodes/month or documented capture on prior screening PSG) increases the probability of event yield but shrinks the eligible pool and may delay enrollment past year-one milestones. Conversely, relaxing criteria to the current ≥2/month threshold broadens recruitment but trades against the protocol ceiling of 20% unproductive admissions—if hit, the facility must pause and redesign, consuming time and budget during a critical validation window.
options: Maintain the current ≥2 self-reported episodes per month threshold and implement a mandatory pre-admission screening PSG 1–3 months before facility admission to identify participants with higher intrinsic capture likelihood, documenting at least one event on that screening study before enrollment.

Require specialist confirmation of a clear parasomnia history plus collateral informant account, but explicitly exclude participants with prior failed research admissions or isolated childhood-only parasomnias, accepting a narrower recruitment funnel to reduce unproductive stays.

Accept the ≥2/month criterion with no screening PSG requirement, but implement a structured escalation protocol: first 2-week stay is diagnostic only; if zero events captured, a second 2-week stay occurs with protocol-permitted timing adjustments (e.g., sleep restriction or alcohol washout per AASM guidance) before participant discharge.
review: The tension is between enrollment speed (needed to hit 50–70 participants by month 36) and event-capture reliability (the Aim 2 phenotyping analysis requires a prespecified minimum number of adjudicated events). All three options accept the risk that some participants will complete extended stays with no captured events, but none addresses the staffing and site-management burden if the facility fills beds with low-yield participants—a 20% unproductive ceiling may be breached silently over the first 18 months before the external review trigger activates.

name: Annotation and Scoring Workflow Architecture
consequences: Manual dual-scoring of all night-length recordings by independent raters (current plan) is scientifically rigorous but creates a bottleneck: at full capacity (35 participants, 2–8 week stays), roughly 245–490 participant-nights per year must be scored—at ~4 hours per night per scorer, this requires ~2 FTE dedicated scoring staff. Outsourcing scoring to a contracted neurophysiology center reduces internal overhead but introduces variability in AASM criterion application and may degrade inter-rater reliability if not tightly supervised.
options: Deploy a two-tier scoring model: technician real-time annotation flags candidate events nightly; a single senior neurophysiologist scores all flagged segments plus 15% random non-flagged segments to detect missed events, and a second rater independently scores 100% of flagged events only, reporting inter-rater agreement stratified by event type.

Contract a certified sleep-scoring service (e.g., a regional neurophysiology center with AASM-accredited scorers) to score 100% of enhanced nights and escalation nights, while keeping night-technician annotation and dual-scoring in-house only for the final dataset used in Aim 2 and Aim 3 analyses, reducing internal FTE burden but risking score harmonization drift.

Establish an in-house second scorer role as a 0.5 FTE postdoctoral researcher who also contributes to event-triage algorithm development, creating a dual-purpose position that adds publication leverage and methodological consistency but concentrates scoring dependency on a single individual and tightens the postdoc's research bandwidth.
review: The core tension is between annotation fidelity (dual independent scoring is essential for inter-rater reliability, a key Aim 1 success metric) and operational cost (scoring is labor-intensive and hard to automate). All three options maintain dual scoring for adjudication but none addresses the scenario where parasomnia events are inherently ambiguous—for example, a confusional arousal may present as brief mumbling vs. complex motor behavior, and two raters might disagree on AASM criteria boundaries; the options lack a formal consensus or appeal protocol for genuinely equivocal events.

name: Participant Stay Duration and Flexible Scheduling
consequences: The current plan permits 2–8 week stays with extension to 10 weeks total if zero events are captured. Shorter, fixed stays (e.g., 2 weeks maximum) accelerate participant turnover and reduce bed-blocking costs but increase the proportion of participants discharged without event capture, risking the 20% unproductive ceiling. Longer, more flexible stays maximize event yield but extend participant compensation costs, staff scheduling complexity, and psychological burden on research subjects isolated in a residential facility.
options: Fix all initial stays at 3 weeks, with no extensions offered; participants who have not experienced a captured event are invited to reapply for a second 3-week admission after a 1-month washout, decoupling episode capture from individual-stay duration and allowing staff to cycle participants in a regular rhythm.

Offer individualized stay length negotiation during pre-admission screening based on participant-reported episode frequency and employment status: high-frequency participants (≥4/month) are offered 2-week stays, standard participants (2–4/month) are offered 3–4 weeks, and low-frequency participants may opt for a single 6-week stay or two separate 3-week stays with a 2-month gap, trading administrative overhead for higher recruitment appeal.

Implement a performance-based extension protocol: all participants begin with a 2-week stay; at day 10, technician and PI review event capture progress; if zero events and participant consent, an automatic 1-week extension is offered at no additional compensation cost to the participant, with a hard cutoff at 4 weeks to control costs while preserving a data-driven second chance.
review: The tension is between cost control (longer stays and extensions increase per-participant expense) and event yield (parasomnia episodes are rare and may cluster unpredictably, so fixed stay lengths risk missing the phenomena). None of the options explicitly accounts for seasonal or circadian variation in parasomnia frequency—for instance, if most participants' events cluster in the early morning hours on specific nights, a 3-week stay may miss seasonal trends entirely, and the options do not propose dynamic protocols that adjust based on preliminary data from the first cohort.

name: Technology Stack Maturity and Sensor Dependency
consequences: The tiered acquisition model (low-burden headbands + contact-free mattress sensors + infrared cameras + ambient microphones + escalation to full PSG) relies on seamless time-synchronization and EEG-BIDS-compatible storage. If headband or mattress-sensor reliability degrades (e.g., dry electrodes lose contact, mattress pressure sensors drift), the unit loses the low-burden baseline capture and must escalate all nights to full PSG montage, driving up costs and annotation burden. Early equipment failures during the pilot year could delay validation milestones and jeopardize month-18 enrollment targets.
options: Conduct a 4-week equipment validation trial at University Hospital Bonn's sleep lab before facility admission begins, documenting headband signal quality, mattress-sensor accuracy, infrared motion detection, and time-synchronization latency across representative patient populations and environmental conditions, with pass-fail thresholds defined by the PI and data engineer before any participant recruitment.

Procure all sensing hardware with redundancy: dual headband units per participant (active backup if primary fails), two independent contact-free mattress sensors per suite, and dual infrared camera-microphone systems with automatic failover, adding ~€50K to capital equipment costs but eliminating single-point-of-failure risk during the pilot year.

Adopt a pragmatic two-phase sensor strategy: year one uses only established PSG montage (scalp EEG, EOG, chin EMG, leg EMG) on all nights, treating the contact-free sensors as optional research extensions with minimal operational dependency; after 12 months, assess headband and mattress-sensor maturity and decide whether to integrate them into routine workflow, deferring sensor-stack complexity until operational stability is proven.
review: The tension is between technological ambition (tiered sensing is scientifically elegant and reduces participant burden) and operational risk (immature sensor technology can fail silently or drift, corrupting the very naturalistic-capture advantage the facility seeks). All three options assume that if a sensor fails, the fallback is manual review or traditional PSG, but none addresses the scenario where headband or mattress-sensor data quality is subtly degraded—e.g., artifacts that resemble parasomnia-related movements—introducing systematic bias into event-triage algorithm training data without immediate detection.

name: Event-Triage Algorithm Scope and Benchmark Rigor
consequences: Aim 3 proposes a semi-automated event-triage tool that ranks probable parasomnia episodes to reduce manual review burden. If the algorithm achieves high sensitivity (catches most real events), manual review volume drops but false-positive alerts may frustrate technicians. If tuned for high specificity (few false alarms), valuable events might be missed and never flagged for full PSG escalation. The benchmark—dual human scorer agreement—is sound but assumes human raters are truth; in reality, atypical parasomnia presentations may be genuinely ambiguous, and algorithm performance may asymptote below human agreement.
options: Design the event-triage algorithm to maximize sensitivity at the cost of higher false-positive rates (e.g., threshold set so algorithm recommends escalation for ~70% of candidate segments), then measure how many technician-reviewed alerts lead to confirmed parasomnia events vs. wasted review time, accepting that some false positives are acceptable cost of ensuring no real events are missed.

Build the algorithm with a multi-class output structure that ranks segments as high-probability parasomnia, low-probability parasomnia, normal arousal, and non-event, then require technician confirmation only for high-probability segments, using technician feedback to iteratively refine class boundaries across the pilot year with no expectation of full automation.

Develop the algorithm as a secondary research tool applied post-hoc to archived data for publication purposes (Aim 3 deliverable), but do not integrate it into the real-time operational workflow during the facility's first 2 years; instead, rely entirely on technician flagging and manual scoring to preserve data quality and inter-rater reliability during the methodological-validation phase, deferring operational deployment to a phase-two expansion grant.
review: The tension is between operational efficiency (automation reduces technician burden and accelerates data annotation) and methodological rigor (early algorithm deployment on incomplete or noisy training data risks learning parasomnia-irrelevant patterns and degrading Aim 3 validity). All three options accept that the algorithm will be benchmarked against dual human scoring, but none grapples with the problem that the facility's recruitment and event-yield constraints may produce an algorithmically unbalanced training set—e.g., if the facility over-enrolls participants with frequent, stereotyped episodes, the algorithm may fail on rare or atypical presentations encountered in clinical use, and the initial validation cohort size may be too small to expose this generalization gap.

name: Pharmaceutical Partnership Structure and Independence Risk
consequences: The plan positions a pharma partnership as optional upside with the academic team retaining full publication rights and the partner receiving only a 30-day pre-publication comment window. This protects academic independence but creates ambiguity: if a pharma partner sponsors data collection targeting a specific parasomnia phenotype or drug candidate, the facility's recruitment strategy and protocol design may drift toward pharma interests. Conversely, rejecting pharma funding increases pressure on DFG and university sources and may force budget cuts or reduced participant compensation, affecting recruitment.
options: Establish a formal written agreement with any pharma partner before facility launch, specifying: no partner-directed recruitment criteria beyond the study's core inclusion/exclusion, no pharma veto over publication timing or content, explicit ownership of data by the university, and a capped research fee (e.g., €200K per year maximum) that funds only incremental data collection, not core operations, with an annual ethics review affirming no pharma influence has altered study aims.

Pursue pharma partnerships only after Aim 1 validation is complete (month 18–24), when the facility's operational model and data quality are proven; delay partnership discussions until then, avoiding early entanglement during the methodological-validation phase and allowing the academic team to establish independent publication momentum.

Decline pharma partnerships entirely for the 3-year DFG grant period, relying exclusively on DFG and university funding, with a commitment to approach potential pharma collaborators only for a proposed phase-two expansion grant after initial findings are published, preserving complete operational independence during the critical facility-establishment window.
review: The tension is between financial sustainability (pharma partnerships can top up budgets and offset enrollment shortfalls) and research independence (pharma interests in specific drug candidates or phenotypes can invisibly influence protocol design). All three options assume that if pharma funds data collection, the published data will eventually be unambiguously attributable to academic investigators; however, none addresses the scenario where pharma funding supports infrastructure or staff time in a way that makes disentangling their influence difficult—for example, if a pharma partner funds a postdoctoral researcher with implicit interest in a particular parasomnia subtype, publication bias toward that subtype becomes hard to audit and hard to disclose.

name: Data Sharing and Repository Strategy
consequences: The plan restricts video data from public repositories due to privacy constraints, but deposits de-identified physiological and sensor data under data use agreements. This maximizes reproducibility for algorithms and phenotyping work but creates an asymmetry: other laboratories cannot inspect video-derived event annotations alongside the raw physiological data, limiting transparency for Aim 3 algorithm benchmarking. Negotiating individual data use agreements (instead of deposit in open repositories like OpenNeuro) also reduces discoverability and slows secondary research use.
options: Deposit all de-identified physiological and sensor streams in OpenNeuro or a similar BIDS-compatible public repository with open access; video data and video-derived annotations remain in a university-hosted institutional repository with access by request to vetted researchers under a data use agreement that explicitly permits algorithm development and testing but prohibits video redistribution.

Implement a tiered access model with three levels: open access for de-identified sensor-derived event tables and aggregate statistics (timestamps, event duration, frequency per participant, sleep stage context); university-managed restricted access for complete raw physiological streams and corresponding annotations for researchers who sign a data use agreement; and no public access to video data or video-annotated waveforms, reserving these for the core research team only.

Negotiate a phased release schedule: deposit initial physiological dataset (year-one and year-two cohorts) in OpenNeuro 6 months after manuscript publication; delay year-three dataset deposit until after all planned publications are complete (month 42+); communicate the deposit timeline transparently in the open-access manuscript so secondary researchers can anticipate data availability and plan replication studies accordingly.
review: The tension is between data openness (open repositories accelerate secondary research and strengthen reproducibility) and privacy protection (video data cannot be meaningfully anonymized and open access risks participant re-identification through behavioral patterns). All three options balance these constraints but none fully resolves the underlying problem that the facility's scientific advantage—capturing naturalistic parasomnia in a domestic environment via video—is exactly the modality that cannot be openly shared; consequently, the event-triage algorithm's training data (video-annotated polysomnography) will remain largely private, limiting independent algorithm benchmarking and potentially inflating reported performance metrics relative to external validation.

name: Participant Selection Stringency versus Phenotypic Diversity
consequences: Tightening inclusion criteria (e.g., requiring ≥4 episodes per month, documented prior PSG evidence of events, or caregiver corroboration) reduces admission of borderline cases and likely boosts event-capture rates, but narrows the study sample to a more homogeneous, treatment-seeking population and may exclude milder or less-chronicled presentations that are scientifically interesting. Looser criteria maximize representativeness and recruitment flow but risk a higher proportion of admissions with genuinely low event frequency, elevating the unproductive-admission rate and straining annotation resources.
options: Implement a two-tier screening protocol with a brief in-person sleep history and actigraphy pre-screening phase before residential admission, admitting only participants whose documented frequency and severity meet quantitative thresholds (e.g., ≥3 events per month confirmed on review), reducing borderline cases.

Maintain the current clinical-interview-based adjudication pathway with loose quantitative gates, then stratify the analysis post-hoc by event frequency and phenotype subgroup, accepting higher initial admission heterogeneity to capture the full clinical spectrum.

Recruit a stratified cohort with explicit quotas for three severity tiers (low, moderate, high event frequency per self-report and caregiver report), then analyze each tier separately to preserve generalizability while managing annotation burden through transparent subgroup design.
review: The core tension is recruitment speed versus data yield: tighter selection accelerates event-capture targets but may require a narrower referral funnel that slows enrollment and limits generalizability to real-world parasomnia populations. None of the options directly address the question of how to rapidly identify misreported or exaggerated event histories during intake, which is a common challenge in rare-disease recruitment; a third-party verification step (e.g., brief phone interview with bed partner) is missing from all pathways.

name: Tiered Sensor Deployment Timing and Escalation Logic
consequences: The current plan specifies scheduled enhanced PSG nights (first, mid, and final) plus technician-triggered escalation, but does not formally define when and how a technician flags escalation or how many escalation nights are budgeted per participant. Moving to a fully scheduled high-density protocol (e.g., PSG on every night or every other night) captures more physiological context but multiplies electrode-placement burden, disrupts the domestic feel, and strains annotation capacity. Conversely, purely reactive escalation (only after a flagged event) risks missing the physiological substrate of undetected episodes and may bias the dataset toward high-visibility events (e.g., loud vocalizations) that trigger technician awareness.
options: Establish a rule-based escalation protocol in which a technician flags escalation when low-burden sensors (mattress pressure, infrared motion, or audio amplitude thresholds) cross prespecified boundaries, then deploy full PSG within one hour, with a budget cap of one escalation night per participant per week to maintain annotation feasibility.

Schedule full PSG on a staggered cadence during weeks 1, 3, 5, and final week of each stay to ensure temporal coverage of acclimation, mid-stay, and terminal phases, reducing technician decision burden but accepting a fixed cost regardless of observed event density.

Reserve high-density PSG deployment only for the first two admissions of each participant (initial and a repeat stay weeks later), using those intensive recordings to establish a phenotypic profile, then revert to low-burden monitoring with reactive escalation for subsequent stays, balancing early-stage precision against long-term operational efficiency.
review: The tension is between capturing complete physiological context and avoiding annotation fatigue; escalation rules are intuitive in principle but depend on sensor sensitivity and technician expertise that may drift over the 3-year project. The options do not adequately address the cost and clinical-feasibility trade-off of escalating at midnight or during the technician's lowest-alertness window; a shift-based escalation protocol (e.g., escalations only during core sleep hours 23:00–06:00) might be operationally necessary but is not discussed.

name: Annotation Task Distribution and Inter-Rater Reliability Maintenance
consequences: The plan calls for dual-rater annotation and inter-rater reliability reporting, but does not specify whether the two raters are the night technician (who observes real-time context) plus a blinded off-site scorer, or both fully blinded, or one senior and one junior with re-training cadence. Real-time technician scoring is fast and context-aware but risks observer bias; blinded dual-rating is methodologically stricter but requires hiring and training a second independent scorer and managing their schedule. Inconsistent rater training or turnover of the second rater erodes reliability reporting mid-project, undermining Aim 3 benchmarking.
options: Deploy a two-stage annotation workflow where the on-site night technician performs real-time event flagging and preliminary classification using a simplified checklist, then a single off-site sleep medicine physician (blinded to technician notes) performs full AASM-criteria scoring on all flagged nights with inter-rater reliability calculated between technician flags and physician scores, creating a hybrid model that balances speed with independent verification.

Hire and retain a dedicated second scorer (0.5 FTE sleep technologist or physiologist) specifically for annotation, with both the technician and the dedicated scorer independently rating all nights double-blind, meeting monthly for calibration sessions and rechecking agreement every 50 scored nights, ensuring strict dual-rater independence but incurring fixed overhead.

Outsource all annotation to a specialized sleep-scoring service (e.g., a commercial polysomnography reading center with published reliability metrics), providing training data from the first 10 participants and then submitting all subsequent nights for remote scoring, eliminating internal annotation capacity risk but sacrificing real-time quality control and creating a dependency on external vendor responsiveness.
review: The core tension is independence of judgment versus operational efficiency: dedicated in-house dual scoring maximizes control and reproducibility but locks in labor costs; outsourcing reduces internal burden but introduces vendor dependency and may delay scoring feedback needed for real-time escalation decisions. None of the options addresses the training cost for a second rater unfamiliar with parasomnia or the risk that a single off-site physician's scoring standard may drift over 3 years without regular in-person calibration meetings.

name: Domestic Ambiance Preservation versus Safety and Monitoring Density
consequences: The plan specifies padded corridors, restricted windows, and silent door alarms to balance safety with a domestic feel, but increasing participant numbers and extended stays may require additional safety infrastructure (e.g., more door monitors, motion sensors, or staff presence) that degrades the informal residential character and potentially suppresses the naturalistic parasomnia behavior that justifies the residential model over an outpatient protocol. Conversely, minimizing overt safety measures risks an adverse event (e.g., a participant sustaining a fall or leaving the facility undetected) that would halt operations and trigger regulatory scrutiny.
options: Adopt a zoned safety model in which communal areas and bedrooms maintain a residential aesthetic with minimal visible monitoring, while a dedicated 24-hour staffing presence (one technician per 4 active suites on night shift) provides real-time observation and rapid response, backed by silent motion-sensor alerts only in high-risk areas such as stairwells and exterior exits.

Install discrete but comprehensive motion-capture and contact-sensor arrays throughout the facility with real-time alerts to staff, accepting a slight increase in perceived clinical atmosphere in exchange for continuous automated monitoring that reduces reliance on staffing density and enables rapid event detection even during low-staff periods.

Implement a tiered admission protocol in which lower-acuity participants (e.g., confusional arousals without wandering history) stay in a minimally monitored domestic wing with sporadic staff checks, while higher-risk cases (e.g., those with injury history or frequent sleepwalking) occupy a separate monitored wing with denser sensor and staffing presence, segregating aesthetic trade-offs by risk profile.
review: The fundamental tension is between incident prevention and ecological validity: the facility cannot simultaneously be a completely unsupervised home and a zero-risk clinical environment. The options sidestep the question of whether additional safety measures should be visible and transparent to participants (potentially affecting behavior) or covert (preserving ambiance but raising informed-consent and trust issues); a hybrid model that explicitly discusses safety with participants and leverages their buy-in is absent from all three options.

name: Capital Expansion Timing and Pathway to 12 Suites
consequences: The plan defers expansion beyond 8 suites until pilot validation is complete, minimizing sunk capital and financial risk if Aim 1 milestones are not met. However, delaying expansion until month 24 means the facility cannot scale enrollment or event-capture throughput until year 2 or 3, potentially missing early publication windows and reducing the per-participant amortized cost of fixed overhead (facility rent, utilities, core staff salaries). Conversely, front-loading investment in 12 suites at launch (either full renovation or holding lease contingency) accelerates scale and reduces per-unit operational cost but commits capital upfront and assumes pilot success without evidence.
options: Proceed with the planned 8-suite launch and defer all expansion decisions to month 24, when Aim 1 validation data (safety, staffing ratios, event-capture rates) are complete; if success criteria are met, initiate 12-suite renovation in year 3 with a second capital request, accepting slower enrollment growth but minimizing upfront financial commitment.

Secure a larger property or lease expansion option at the outset, completing full infrastructure and regulatory approval for 12 suites by month 12 (in parallel with 8-suite pilot), then activate the additional 4 suites on a rolling basis as staffing and recruitment readiness permit, reducing future expansion friction but incurring renovation costs during year 1 with only 8-suite revenue.

Negotiate a modular lease and equipment strategy that allows 8-suite operation at launch with a formal break clause at month 18, then pivot at that point to either expanding to 12 suites (committing to the larger footprint) or consolidating to a smaller dedicated facility if pilot results indicate lower-than-expected event yield or operational complexity, retaining maximum flexibility at the cost of administrative overhead.
review: The core tension is capital efficiency versus enrollment velocity: investing early in 12-suite capacity de-risks future expansion but may strand expensive infrastructure if event-capture rates or recruitment fall short of projections. The options do not adequately address the lease-negotiation leverage available in year 1 (when a landlord is motivated to lock in long-term occupancy) versus year 2–3 (when renegotiation is harder); a strategic lease-negotiation angle that frontloads optionality terms without requiring immediate build-out is not explored.

name: Pharmaceutical Partnership Sequencing and Governance Independence
consequences: The plan positions pharma partnerships as optional upside with a 30-day pre-publication comment window and full publication rights retained by the academic team, reducing feasibility dependence on industry while opening potential co-funding. However, early partnership engagement (year 1) could accelerate recruitment through pharma-sponsored participant outreach or provide additional sensor or personnel funding, whereas delaying partnership until results are preliminary (year 2+) reduces influence on study design but may narrow pharma interest to confirmatory work only. A poorly timed partnership or one with unstated expectations (e.g., pharma assuming first authorship or data exclusivity) can derail publication timelines and alienate the external advisory board.
options: Solicit a formal pharma partnership during year 1 with a signed collaboration agreement specifying publication rights, authorship tiers, data use restrictions, and a 30-day pre-publication review clause, leveraging early partnership funding to augment recruitment and sensor infrastructure while maintaining governance autonomy via the external advisory board.

Defer pharma outreach until month 18, after Aim 1 validation and the first methods-validation manuscript are near-complete, positioning the academic team as having demonstrated scientific rigor and reducing pharma's ability to impose protocol changes, but accepting slower access to co-funding and narrower alignment with pharma development timelines.

Establish a separate Investigator-Initiated-Trial (IIT) funding mechanism through which individual participating pharmaceutical companies can sponsor post-hoc analyses of existing cohort data without influencing the main study design, creating a subsidiary revenue stream that does not obligate the core protocol but allows flexible engagement at different pharma involvement levels.
review: The core tension is autonomy versus funding sustainability: early pharma partnerships provide capital and recruitment leverage but create expectations and power imbalances; late partnerships preserve independence but may reduce pharma interest to salvage analyses only. The options do not address the reputational risk to the academic institution if a pharma partner later discloses financial disputes or if the partnership is perceived as biasing sample selection (e.g., enriching for a pharma-target phenotype); a transparent governance structure with documented decision-making visibility is not specified.

name: Publication Strategy and Longitudinal Phenotyping Pace
consequences: The 3-year project success criteria require 2–3 peer-reviewed publications, but the timing and content of publications directly affect enrollment momentum, regulatory credibility, and grant-renewal competitiveness. Publishing a rapid methods-validation paper (year 1–2) establishes the facility's scientific legitimacy and attracts referrals, but may appear premature to high-impact venues and divulges methodological details that competitors could adopt. Withholding publications until comprehensive longitudinal data are available (year 2–3) maximizes scientific depth but risks losing visibility and may delay grant-renewal decisions based on demonstrated productivity.
options: Adopt a staged publication strategy with a methods-validation manuscript targeting a methodology-focused journal (e.g., Sleep) by month 18, a longitudinal phenotyping preprint deposited at month 24, and a final peer-reviewed characterization paper submitted by month 36, prioritizing early visibility and regular dissemination over impact-factor maximization.

Pursue a single high-impact synthesis manuscript by month 30 that integrates methods validation, longitudinal phenotyping, and semi-automated event-triage benchmarking into a unified narrative suitable for a top-tier journal, delaying publication but maximizing visibility and citation impact, accepting the risk that journal review timelines may push actual publication past the 36-month window.

Publish low-barrier preliminary findings (e.g., recruitment and retention data, adverse-event documentation, baseline sensor-agreement metrics) in a specialized sleep research journal or conference proceedings by month 12, then reserve the main longitudinal and phenotyping analysis for month 24–36, segmenting outputs by scientific scope to maintain publication momentum while protecting the most novel phenotyping findings for high-impact venues.
review: The core tension is publication velocity versus scientific maturity: early publication builds momentum and credibility but risks underselling methodological novelty or phenotyping findings to narrow audiences; late publication maximizes depth but compresses the timeline for grant-renewal decisions and risks scooping by other parasomnia research groups. None of the options addresses the challenge of balancing open-science data release (required for some funders) with the desire to publish novel findings exclusively; a data embargo or preprint-first strategy is not discussed.

name: Pre-Admission Screening Depth versus Recruitment Timeline
consequences: A rigorous multi-step screening (specialist interview, bed-partner collateral, prior PSG, exclusion adjudication) ensures participant homogeneity and reduces false admissions, but lengthens the pre-enrollment period and may deter borderline candidates. A lighter screening front-loaded by the sleep clinic alone accelerates intake but risks enrolling mild or atypical cases that contaminate phenotyping datasets and consume facility capacity without capturing definitive events.
options: Require all candidates to undergo a dedicated 90-minute structured PI interview plus independent neurologist review before bed assignment, maintaining diagnostic precision at the cost of 2–3 week delays and potential attrition among self-referred participants.

Accept sleep clinic referrals with a brief telephonic pre-screening and admit within 1 week, conducting full clinical adjudication during the first overnight stay and using the enhanced-night PSG as the definitive diagnostic test.

Implement a two-tier screening: rapid telephone screen for red flags (epilepsy history, active substance use, severe sleep apnea) followed by admission for provisionally eligible participants, with formal diagnostic adjudication deferred to 48 hours post-arrival based on baseline sensor and clinical data.
review: Rigorous pre-screening ensures diagnostic homogeneity but delays enrollment and contradicts the goal of capturing naturalistic behavior in borderline phenotypes. Lighter screening accelerates throughput but inflates the unproductive-admission rate, risking the 20% protocol ceiling and triggering a recruitment-criteria review mid-trial. None of the options address the tension between early diagnostic certainty and the possibility that some participants may evolve into frank parasomnia during the stay—a longitudinal insight that pure pre-selection filters out.

name: Night-Shift Staffing Model and Incident-Response Capacity
consequences: The plan specifies 3 technicians rotating coverage for 8 active suites, enabling adequate response time to flagged events and annotation throughput. Expanding to 12 suites without hiring a 4th technician stretches response latency, increases annotation delays, and raises the risk of missing escalation-night PSG setup. Conversely, hiring excess staff ahead of occupancy accumulates idle-wage burden under German employment law and strains the €2M personnel budget.
options: Hire a 4th technician before expansion to 12 suites, accepting a 3–6 month wage burden during the ramp-up phase but ensuring event response time remains under 5 minutes and annotation backlog stays below one suite per night.

Scale technician hiring strictly to census, hiring the 4th technician only once suites 9–10 are consistently occupied, allowing cost flexibility but introducing a 2–3 week capacity lag where response time may exceed safe thresholds.

Cross-train clinical psychologist and study coordinator in basic night technician duties (event flagging, preliminary annotation, PSG lead placement) to create a flexible reserve pool for high-occupancy nights without permanent headcount increase.
review: Hiring ahead of demand avoids operational bottlenecks but locks in fixed costs during a pilot phase when enrollment may plateau. Hiring strictly to census saves money initially but creates a capacity crunch during surge periods when participants cluster in early cohorts. The third option assumes cross-training is compatible with clinical and administrative roles under German labor regulations—but if labor law mandates separate employment contracts or if the psychologist's primary clinical duties are non-negotiable, this option becomes infeasible without creating permanent part-time roles that carry their own overhead.

name: Escalation-Night Triggered PSG Cadence and Technician Annotation Load
consequences: The current protocol mandates scheduled enhanced nights (night 1, mid-stay, final) plus technician-triggered escalation PSG after flagged events. Technician annotations require real-time scoring during the night and later verification by two independent raters, consuming annotation labor proportional to event capture rate. A more restrictive escalation policy (e.g., only after two confirmed events per week) reduces PSG electrode setup frequency but may miss rare or clustered episodes. A more liberal policy (PSG after every high-confidence flag) maximizes clinical-grade data but overwhelms annotation capacity and balloons equipment wear and maintenance costs.
options: Maintain the current tri-schedule plus single-event escalation rule, accepting that annotation will require approximately 4–6 person-hours per captured event and targeting completion within 7 days of data collection.

Establish a threshold-based escalation trigger: deploy full PSG only after technician flags a minimum of two distinct episodes within a rolling 7-day window, reducing PSG frequency by an estimated 30–40% and annotation load proportionally.

Automate preliminary event detection via the dry-electrode headband and mattress sensor fusion pipeline, alerting the technician to high-confidence candidates in real time and reserving full PSG deployment for events that meet both automated and manual confirmation criteria.
review: Generous escalation capture maximizes clinical data richness but strains the team's annotation capacity—if two raters spend 6+ hours per event and the facility captures 15 events per month, annotation backlog becomes a serializing bottleneck. Restrictive escalation reduces annotation burden but risks missing the heterogeneity and temporal clustering that define longitudinal phenotyping. The third option (automated pre-screening) is conceptually sound but assumes the semi-automated triage algorithm will be reliable before the system is fully operational—using an unproven algorithm to gate PSG deployment introduces circularity and delays the validation of the triage model itself.

name: Matched Control Group Recruitment and Naturalistic Baseline Definition
consequences: A matched control cohort (identical monitoring, no parasomnia history) provides a critical benchmark for sensor false-positive rates and baseline nocturnal movement patterns, enabling the event-triage algorithm to distinguish normal arousal and movement from pathological parasomnia. Recruiting and scheduling controls alongside clinical participants complicates logistics—controls must stay 2–8 weeks while healthy, which is harder to incentivize than treating a clinical population. Conversely, omitting controls forces reliance on historical normative data or published baselines, which may not reflect the specific sensor suite and domestic environment in Bonn.
options: Enroll a separate dedicated control cohort (n=15–20) on a fixed annual schedule, coordinated with participant recruitment to ensure temporal alignment and sensor configuration consistency across both groups.

Recruit controls opportunistically and on demand—prioritizing family members or close associates of enrolled parasomnia participants who share the domestic environment and can provide within-household baseline data.

Implement a rolling control design: admit one control participant every 2–3 months on a regular cadence to maintain continuous baseline reference data without the scheduling complexity of a large dedicated cohort.
review: A large dedicated control group guarantees robust normative data but requires motivating healthy adults to spend weeks in a research facility, competing with clinical recruitment timelines. Opportunistic family-member controls introduce confounding—participants sleeping in the same house may share circadian, environmental, or genetic influences on arousal patterns. The rolling design smooths logistics but provides sparse, discontinuous baseline reference points that may not capture seasonal variation or equipment drift. None address the possibility that controls may develop subclinical parasomnia during their stay, blurring the clinical-normal boundary.

name: Physiological Data Retention, Deletion, and Long-Term Repository Strategy
consequences: BIDS-compatible de-identified data deposited in a recognized public repository (e.g., OpenNeuro, dbGaP) enables future meta-analyses and external algorithm testing, supporting the field's cumulative knowledge. However, retaining raw physiological data—especially video and audio—indefinitely raises privacy and GDPR compliance costs, requires redundant storage infrastructure, and creates legal liability if a breach occurs. Aggressive deletion (e.g., after 5 years or after publication) reduces liability but forecloses reanalysis under future analytical methods and external validation.
options: Deposit de-identified physiological streams (EEG, EMG, movement, heart rate) in a recognized repository at study completion, apply tiered consent for video/audio (retain only with explicit participant re-consent after de-identification audit), and delete all video/audio from local systems after 7 years or first publication, whichever is later.

Maintain full de-identified datasets (including sensor-derived video metadata but excluding raw video) on university-controlled servers indefinitely, with access restricted via formal data use agreements and institutional review, avoiding public repository dependency and centralizing privacy governance.

Create a federated architecture: deposit EEG and validated event annotations in a public repository immediately post-publication, retain raw sensor data (video, audio, movement) in a university-managed secure enclave for 10 years to enable internal reanalysis and external researchers' on-site access only, then purge all retained media.
review: Full public deposit maximizes scientific impact but intensifies privacy risk from video and audio streams despite de-identification. Long-term university retention centralizes privacy control but reduces external scrutiny, creates indefinite storage costs, and may discourage data sharing despite stated openness. Federated separation allows immediate publication of key data while protecting privacy-sensitive streams, but creates an artificial asymmetry—external researchers cannot verify de-identification rigor on the withheld video, undermining trust in published annotations.

name: Pharmaceutical Partnership Timing and Data-Access Governance Framework
consequences: Early pharma engagement (year 1) provides co-funding and pharmaceutical-grade data governance infrastructure, accelerating buildout and reducing DFG dependence. However, partner presence may bias recruitment (selective enrollment of participants with specific phenotypes relevant to drug development), constrain publication timing through the 30-day comment window, or shift scientific focus toward pharma interests. Late engagement (year 2–3) preserves autonomy and allows the team to establish independent methods and publications first, but pharma funding becomes optional upside rather than enabling capital, and the partner may demand priority data access or co-authorship provisions retrospectively.
options: Approach pharmaceutical partners only after the facility demonstrates 24+ months of safe operations, enrollment of 40+ participants, and 1–2 publications, positioning the partner as a scale-up funder for years 2–3 and requiring formal data-access and publication-governance agreements upfront.

Invite a pharmaceutical partner to join as a non-voting scientific collaborator from month 6, providing early exposure to protocol design and preliminary data, with a formal legal clause guaranteeing the academic team's unilateral publication rights and the pharma partner's 30-day pre-publication comment window only.

Structure pharmaceutical partnership as a separate arm or ancillary study with distinct enrollment cohorts (e.g., pharma-selected participants with specific genotypes or comorbidities) conducted in parallel to the core academic study, isolating potential bias and allowing the core protocol to remain pure.
review: Early partnership brings resources but risks contaminating recruitment and publication strategy with commercial interests, particularly if the partner defaults to seeking exclusivity or expedited data hand-off. Late partnership preserves academic autonomy but leaves the team financially vulnerable in year 2–3 if DFG funding lags. Segregating partnership into a separate arm duplicates infrastructure and staffing overhead, and creates two distinct cohorts that may limit cross-dataset insights—moreover, if the pharma arm shows superior outcomes, academic credibility of the core study comes into question.

name: Event-Capture Yield Targets and Stay-Duration Flexibility Thresholds
consequences: The protocol assumes 70–80% of admitted participants will produce at least one adjudicated event during a 2–8 week stay, with optional extension to 10 weeks and a 20% unproductive-admission ceiling. Holding this ceiling strictly ensures operational throughput and constrains facility occupancy unpredictably—if 4 of the first 20 admissions yield zero events, protocol review is triggered. Relaxing the ceiling (e.g., 25% or 30%) accommodates participant variance and protects against premature protocol revision but may mask recruitment-criteria issues or sensor sensitivity problems. Alternatively, shortening initial stays to 2 weeks and reserving extension for high-confidence cases avoids overstaying unproductive participants and frees capacity for new enrollments.
options: Maintain the 20% unproductive-admission ceiling and conduct mandatory external review if crossed, but simultaneously increase the initial stay window to 2–4 weeks (front-loading more monitoring nights before extension eligibility) to maximize event-capture probability within the standard window and reduce extension requests.

Relax the unproductive-admission ceiling to 25% and implement a dynamic stay-duration algorithm: participants with zero events after two weeks undergo a brief clinical re-interview to rule out criteria misclassification, and extension is offered only to those with subclinical event evidence (detectable sensor signatures or bed-partner reports) suggesting imminent capture.

Set a hard initial-stay limit of 2 weeks for all participants regardless of event yield, then invite only those with strong preliminary evidence of ongoing episodes (technician-detected near-events, video-flagged movement) to return for a second 2–4 week residential block, shifting from continuous stays to episodic return visits and improving facility turnover.
review: Strict adherence to the 20% ceiling and external review ensures methodological accountability but is inflexible if participant variance or seasonal parasomnia patterns cause legitimate yield fluctuations. Relaxing the ceiling introduces drift—where does the line stop? A 30% unproductive rate may still hide recruitment or sensor problems. The third option (episodic return visits) maximizes facility throughput and reduces idle-bed costs but fragments the longitudinal continuity that underlies Aim 2 (within-person pattern characterization), and recall bias may degrade the quality of between-stay event reconstruction.
